perturbation.original_text: "INFORMATION" shall mean any information, technical data or know-how relating to the business, services or products of the disclosing party or a third party, including without limitation any research, products, services, developments, inventions, processes, techniques, designs, components, parts, documents, drawings, electronic files, data sketches, plans, programs, specifications, software, and/or distribution, engineering, marketing, financial, merchandising, sales, and salary information and/or other materials (hereinafter collectively referred to as "INFORMATION") which is disclosed by such party or on its behalf, before or after the date hereof, to the other party or its employees or agents, directly or indirectly, in writing, orally, electronically, or by drawings or inspection.
perturbation.changed_text: "INFORMATION" shall mean any information, technical data or know-how relating to the business, services or products of the disclosing party or a third party, including without limitation any research, products, services, developments, inventions, processes, techniques, designs, components, parts, documents, drawings, electronic files, data sketches, plans, programs, specifications, software, and/or distribution, engineering, marketing, financial, merchandising, sales, and salary information and/or other materials (hereinafter collectively referred to as "INFORMATION") which is disclosed by such party or on its behalf, before or after the date hereof, to the other party or its employees or agents, directly or indirectly, in writing, orally, electronically, or by drawings or inspection. "INFORMATION" also includes any material that the receiving party deems necessary for its own business purposes, regardless of its origin or confidentiality.
perturbation.explanation: The addition of 'INFORMATION' also includes any material that the receiving party deems necessary for its own business purposes, regardless of its origin or confidentiality' directly contradicts the definition of confidential information, potentially allowing the receiving party to claim any information as their own, regardless of source or prior agreements. This creates uncertainty as it voids the point of the NDA
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts the CUTSA's definition of 'trade secret' by including information regardless of its origin or confidentiality. CUTSA requires a trade secret to derive independent economic value from not being generally known and being subject to reasonable efforts to maintain its secrecy. The modified text allows the receiving party to unilaterally declare any information as 'INFORMATION' irrespective of these criteria, effectively nullifying the protection afforded by CUTSA.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.1 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a contradiction by expanding the definition of 'INFORMATION' to include anything the receiving party deems necessary, regardless of origin or confidentiality. This directly opposes the principle that confidential information, such as trade secrets, derives value from being secret and requires reasonable efforts to maintain that secrecy. The added clause allows the receiving party to unilaterally bypass these requirements, essentially nullifying the protections intended by the original agreement. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'Improper means' which includes breach of a duty to maintain secrecy and defines 'Misappropriation' as acquiring or using a trade secret with the knowledge it was acquired by improper means or under circumstances requiring secrecy, principles undermined by the modified definition of information.

perturbation.original_text: The receiving party shall hold in trust and confidence, and not disclose to others, by any means, any and all INFORMATION disclosed under this Agreement. INFORMATION may be used by the receiving party only for the purpose of considering or pursuing a business relationship or business transaction with the disclosing party.
perturbation.changed_text: The receiving party shall endeavor to hold in trust and confidence, and will attempt not to disclose to others, by any means, any and all INFORMATION disclosed under this Agreement. INFORMATION may be used by the receiving party for any purpose it deems beneficial.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall hold' to 'shall endeavor to hold' and 'will attempt not to disclose' weakens the obligation to protect confidential information. Changing 'only for the purpose of considering or pursuing a business relationship or business transaction with the disclosing party' to 'for any purpose it deems beneficial' broadens the scope of permitted use, potentially allowing misuse of confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: While 16600 generally invalidates non-compete agreements, this NDA aims to protect trade secrets. The modification weakens the NDA to such an extent that it no longer adequately protects trade secrets, essentially allowing the receiving party to compete unfairly using the disclosing party's information, undermining the intent to protect confidential information which is related to protection against unfair competition.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The original contract's strict requirements to protect confidential information are significantly weakened in the changed text. The original text states the receiving party 'shall hold in trust and confidence, and not disclose to others' whereas the changed text weakens this to 'shall endeavor to hold in trust and confidence, and will attempt not to disclose to others.' Furthermore, the scope of use of the information broadens from 'only for the purpose of considering or pursuing a business relationship or business transaction with the disclosing party' to 'for any purpose it deems beneficial'. This undermines the protection of trade secrets. BPC 16600 addresses contracts restraining trade but the law explanation argues the changes weaken the NDA to such an extent that it no longer protects trade secrets, potentially allowing unfair competition. Therefore, because 'Misaligned Terminology' is defined as when contractual language deviates from legally recognized definitions, making the contract unenforceable, a legal contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: The receiving party further agrees it shall take the same measures, but no less than reasonable security measures, and use the same care, but no less than a reasonable degree of care, to preserve and protect the secrecy of, and to avoid disclosure or unauthorized use of, the disclosing party's INFORMATION as it uses with its own information of similar importance.
perturbation.changed_text: The receiving party agrees it shall take measures it deems appropriate, and use the care it deems suitable, to preserve and protect the secrecy of, and to avoid disclosure or unauthorized use of, the disclosing party's INFORMATION.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'no less than reasonable security measures' and 'no less than a reasonable degree of care' gives the receiving party complete discretion in how to protect the information, potentially leading to inadequate protection and contradicting the duty of care typically associated with confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: While Section 1708 states that 'Every person is bound, without contract, to abstain from injuring the person or property of another, or infringing upon any of his rights', the removal of 'reasonable measures' and 'reasonable care' weakens the contract's obligation to the point of near uselessness in providing actual protection. Thus, it could be argued the change facilitates, or at least does not sufficiently prevent, injury to the disclosing party's property rights.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1708. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 3. OBLIGATIONS IMPOSED BY LAW [1708 - 1725] ( Part 3 enacted 1872. ) 1708. Every person is bound, without contract, to abstain from injuring the person or property of another, or infringing upon any of his or her rights. (Amended by Stats. 2002, Ch. 664, Sec. 38.5. Effective January 1, 2003.)
perturbation.justification: The original contract language mandates 'no less than reasonable security measures' and 'no less than a reasonable degree of care.' The modified text removes these explicit requirements, granting the receiving party complete discretion without any defined standard of care. This change contradicts California Civil Code Section 1708, which states that 'Every person is bound, without contract, to abstain from injuring the person or property of another, or infringing upon any of his or her rights.' By removing the 'reasonable' standard, the modified contract weakens the protection of the disclosing party's information and could be interpreted as not sufficiently preventing potential harm to their property rights, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The receiving party's duties under Section 2 of this Agreement expire with respect to any particular item of INFORMATION five years after the date of disclosure hereunder to the receiving party, or three years after the expiration or termination of the relationship between the parties to this Agreement, whichever is later.
perturbation.changed_text: The receiving party's duties under Section 2 of this Agreement expire with respect to any particular item of INFORMATION one year after the date of disclosure hereunder to the receiving party, or immediately upon termination of the relationship between the parties to this Agreement, whichever is earlier.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the duration of confidentiality obligations from five years to one year after disclosure, or immediately upon termination, significantly weakens the protection of trade secrets, making them vulnerable to misappropriation sooner.
perturbation.law_explanation: The shortened duration of confidentiality potentially undermines the 'reasonable efforts' requirement of CUTSA. If the confidential information retains economic value for longer than one year and the disclosing party has not implemented more stringent protections than one year, a court might find that they have not taken reasonable steps to protect it, thereby invalidating its trade secret status.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the confidentiality period, which could contradict the 'reasonable efforts' requirement for trade secret protection under California law. Snippet 1 defines 'misappropriation' related to trade secrets, implying a duty to maintain secrecy. The change from five years to one year, or immediate termination of the relationship, may not constitute reasonable efforts if the information retains value longer than the stipulated period, potentially undermining its trade secret status as per the Law Explanation provided.
